verses.commentary: The serpent was more crafty (arum, עָרוּם) than any other beast. Crafty can mean shrewd or cunning. The same root describes the man and woman as naked (arumim) in 2:25, a wordplay suggesting vulnerability. The serpent's first move is to question God's word: Did God really say? Doubt begins with distorting what God actually said.

The woman accurately quotes God's command but adds to it: we must not touch it. God never said this. She builds a fence around the command, perhaps revealing anxiety or a sense that God's boundaries are unreasonable.
She states the consequence: or you will die. The stakes are clear. Death is the result of disobedience. Yet the serpent will challenge even this.
You will not certainly die, the serpent contradicts God directly. The first lie in Scripture. Truth is contested. The serpent doesn't just tempt but accuses God of deception.

God knows that when you eat your eyes will be opened and you will be like God, knowing good and evil. The temptation is not merely fruit but autonomy, the power to define reality apart from God. You will be like God twists the truth that humans already bear God's image. The lie is that they need to grasp what is already given.

The woman saw the fruit was good for food, pleasing to the eye, and desirable for gaining wisdom. The same categories of temptation appear throughout Scripture. She took and ate and gave to her husband who was with her. He was present, silent, passive. Both are complicit. Sin enters through doubt, desire, and disobedience.

Their eyes were opened and they realized they were naked. Knowledge brings shame, not wisdom. They sewed fig leaves together as coverings. The first act after sin is hiding, covering, self-protection. Intimacy is broken. Vulnerability becomes threat.

They heard the sound of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day. God comes seeking them as always. Sin does not drive God away; it drives us into hiding. They hid from the LORD among the trees. The garden that was home becomes a hiding place.
Where are you? God's first question after the fall. Not a request for information but an invitation to confession. God knows where they are. The question exposes their lostness to themselves.

I heard you in the garden and I was afraid because I was naked, so I hid. Fear replaces trust. Shame replaces intimacy. The man names his emotions: heard, afraid, naked, hid. Sin brings fragmentation of self.
Who told you that you were naked? Have you eaten from the tree? God probes the source of shame. The question moves toward the real issue: disobedience. Shame is the symptom, sin is the disease.

The woman you put here with me, she gave me the fruit. The man blames the woman and implicitly God (you put her here). Responsibility is deflected. Relationships fracture. The unity of one flesh becomes accusation.
The serpent deceived me and I ate. The woman blames the serpent. She speaks truth (deception happened) but avoids responsibility (I ate). The blame cascade continues. No one confesses freely.

The LORD God said to the serpent, you are cursed above all livestock and wild animals. The serpent receives a curse directly. You will crawl on your belly and eat dust all your days. Degradation and humiliation. The image may suggest defeat, not biology.

I will put enmity between you and the woman, between your offspring and hers. Ongoing conflict is established. He will crush your head and you will strike his heel. The first gospel promise (protoevangelium): a descendent of the woman will defeat the serpent, though at cost. Victory is promised but through suffering.

To the woman: I will make your pains in childbearing very severe. The blessing to be fruitful (1:28) becomes painful. With painful labor you will give birth. Life-giving becomes life-draining. Your desire will be for your husband and he will rule over you. The relationship becomes distorted by power and control. This describes the brokenness, not God's ideal.

To Adam: Because you listened to your wife and ate, the ground is cursed because of you. The man's vocation (work the ground) becomes toil. Through painful toil you will eat food from it. Work becomes burden, not joy.
It will produce thorns and thistles for you. Resistance, frustration, futility enter human labor. The ground itself works against the gardener.

By the sweat of your brow you will eat your food until you return to the ground. Work becomes exhausting. Death is named directly: you are dust and to dust you will return. Mortality is the consequence. The breath of life (2:7) will be withdrawn. The earth that gave life reclaims the body.

Adam named his wife Eve (Chavah, חַוָּה) because she would become the mother of all the living. Even after judgment, hope remains. Life continues. Her name means life or living. Despite death's sentence, life will go on.

The LORD God made garments of skin and clothed them. God provides covering better than fig leaves. Skin garments require death, the first death of an animal. God's covering requires sacrifice. Grace follows judgment.

The man has become like one of us, knowing good and evil. Ironic fulfillment of the serpent's promise. But autonomy brings death, not life. He must not be allowed to reach out and take from the tree of life and eat and live forever. Eternal life in a fallen state would be eternal curse, not blessing.

The LORD God banished him from the Garden of Eden to work the ground from which he had been taken. Exile from paradise. The vocation continues (work the ground) but outside the garden. Separation from God's immediate presence.

He drove the man out and placed cherubim on the east side with a flaming sword flashing back and forth to guard the way to the tree of life. Cherubim are guardian figures. The return is blocked. Paradise is lost. The way back is guarded. Yet the story is not over. The promise of verse 15 remains.
